name: Gold-Plating Requirements
symptoms: Requirements include features that stakeholders did not explicitly request
BA or developer adds 'nice-to-have' features without business justification
Scope creep driven by technical team's enthusiasm rather than business need
Features are added 'because the technology can do it'
correction: Apply YAGNI principle (You Aren't Gonna Need It). Every requirement must trace to a stakeholder need and measurable business value. Use MoSCoW prioritization and require business sponsor sign-off for all Must-Have items.

name: Vague and Unmeasurable Requirements
symptoms: Requirements use subjective language: 'fast', 'user-friendly', 'easy to use', 'scalable', 'flexible'
No acceptance criteria defined for user stories
Stakeholders can't agree whether the requirement has been met in testing
Requirements like 'the system should be intuitive'
correction: Apply SMART criteria: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Every requirement needs a testable acceptance criterion. Replace 'fast' with 'response time ≤ 2 seconds for 95th percentile of requests under 100 concurrent users'.

name: Scope Creep Without Change Control
symptoms: New requirements are added informally during development without impact analysis
Stakeholders say 'while you're at it, can you also add...'
Project timeline keeps expanding without budget adjustment
Development team complains about constantly shifting requirements
correction: Implement formal change control process: all new requirements after baseline must go through Change Request (CR) form with impact assessment covering cost, schedule, and risk. Steering committee approves/rejects CRs. Document all decisions.

name: Requirements Written as Solutions
symptoms: Requirements describe HOW to build, not WHAT business problem to solve
Example: 'The system must use a SQL database with stored procedures' instead of 'The system must retain transaction history for 7 years'
Technical decisions are embedded in requirements before solution design
Vendor or IT team has already decided technology before requirements are gathered
correction: Use 'shall' language focused on observable system behavior and business outcomes. Keep technology decisions out of requirements documentation. Separate business requirements (what) from solution requirements (how) into distinct sections.

name: Missing Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs)
symptoms: Requirements document contains only functional features, no performance or security specs
No defined SLAs for response times, availability, or throughput
Security requirements absent until penetration test reveals vulnerabilities
GDPR and compliance requirements not captured as hard constraints
correction: Use standard NFR categories as checklist: Performance (response time, throughput), Security (authentication, authorization, encryption), Reliability (uptime SLA, RTO/RPO), Scalability, Compliance (GDPR, industry regulations), Usability, Maintainability. NFRs must be elicited and documented alongside functional requirements.

name: Conflicting Requirements Not Resolved
symptoms: Finance requires all documents retained for 10 years; GDPR requires deletion after 3 years
Sales wants customer portal access to all orders; Security wants data minimization
Two stakeholders give contradictory answers to the same question
Requirements document contains internal contradictions
correction: Document all conflicts explicitly in an open issues register. Escalate to Steering Committee for resolution with clear options and their implications. Never resolve conflicts by ignoring one requirement. Document the decision rationale for audit purposes.

name: Requirements Collected from Wrong Stakeholders
symptoms: Only management interviewed; end users not consulted
IT dictates requirements without business stakeholder involvement
Subject matter experts excluded because 'they're too busy'
Requirements approved by people who won't use the system
correction: Use RACI matrix to identify all stakeholder groups. BABOK stakeholder matrix analysis is mandatory at project start. End users must participate in requirements workshops and user story validation. Approval authority must include those accountable for business outcomes.

name: Requirements Baseline Not Frozen
symptoms: Requirements keep changing after development has started
No formal sign-off exists on requirements baseline
Stakeholders re-open agreed requirements without change control
Development team builds based on email conversations rather than signed-off documents
correction: Establish a formal requirements baseline approval gate before development begins. Use digital signatures or documented meeting minutes as approval evidence. After baseline, all changes must go through Change Request process. Version control requirements documents.

name: Assuming Rather Than Asking
symptoms: BA makes assumptions about stakeholder needs without validation
Phrases like 'users obviously need X' without user confirmation
Design decisions made based on BA's intuition rather than stakeholder input
Important edge cases not addressed because 'that would never happen'
correction: Apply 'assumption busting' technique in requirements workshops. Explicitly document all assumptions made and validate each with relevant stakeholders. Use 'Five Whys' to understand root causes before proposing solutions. Edge cases must be systematically elicited.

name: Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) Not Maintained
symptoms: Cannot determine which features address which business requirements
Test cases exist but don't link to specific requirements
During UAT, stakeholders raise issues about missing features that were never formally required
Post-go-live: scope disputes about what was and wasn't in the contract
correction: Create and maintain RTM throughout the project lifecycle: Business Requirement → Functional Requirement → User Story → Test Case → UAT Test Case. Every requirement must have a test case. RTM review is a mandatory gate at each project phase.

name: Ignoring Integration Requirements
symptoms: Integration points with existing systems treated as afterthoughts
API availability and data format compatibility not verified until development
Requirements document describes standalone functionality but ignores system-to-system data flows
Integration complexity underestimated in project plan
correction: Create interface catalog during requirements phase: for each system interface, document data flows, frequency, format, security requirements, and error handling. Validate technical feasibility with IT before finalizing requirements. Integration requirements often represent 30-40% of total scope.

name: No Definition of Done for Requirements
symptoms: Requirements are marked 'complete' before acceptance criteria are defined
No agreement on what level of detail a requirement must have before development begins
User stories are added to sprint without testing criteria
Team debates what 'done' means during sprint review
correction: Define 'Definition of Ready' for requirements: acceptance criteria written, stakeholder approval obtained, technical feasibility confirmed, dependencies identified. Define 'Definition of Done' for delivery: acceptance criteria met, tested in staging, business stakeholder signed off, documentation updated.
